Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reconsideration Of Ccsfs Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Jul-03-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiff Yalong Dong Motion For Reconsideration Of CCSF's Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings is DENIED.

Plaintiff seeks reconsideration of the court's April 30, 2025 Order under Code of Civil Procedure section 1008. Subdivision (a) of section 1008 requires that reconsideration motions be "based upon new or different facts, circumstances, or law ... [and] shall state by affidavit what application was made before, when and to what judge, what order or decisions were made, and what new or different facts, circumstances, or law are claimed to be shown." (Code of Civ. Proc. 1008(a).) The moving party bears the burden of proving that the alleged "new or different facts, circumstances, or law" could not, with reasonable diligence, have been discovered or produced earlier. (New York Times Co. v. Superior Court (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 206, 212-213.) This standard is jurisdictional. (See Code of Civil Procedure section 1008(e).)

The record on this motion does not establish new or different facts, circumstances, or law that could not, with reasonable diligence, have been discovered or produced in advance of the time the underlying motion was taken under submission. The 2016 Judgment has no obvious relevance to the proper construction of the San Francisco Urban Forestry Ordinance and, specifically, San Francisco Public Works Code section 802's definition of "street tree." Plaintiff contends CCSF should have produced the judgment in discovery, but Plaintiff does not identify any discovery request that called for production of this document. Plaintiff has not demonstrated the "Street Tree SF" publication, which is publicly available, could not, with reasonable diligence, have been discovered before submission of the motion. Plaintiff contends CCSF should have produced the publication in discovery, but, again, Plaintiff does not identify any discovery request that called for production of the publication. Plaintiff has not demonstrated the legal or factual basis for its new common carrier argument could not, with reasonable diligence, have been discovered earlier. Finally, one attorney's health challenges around the time of the motion does not constitute new or different facts or circumstances supporting reconsideration. The court notes that Plaintiff identifies five attorneys as their counsel of record; even if one attorney's health circumstances were a factor, the time to raise this was before, not after, the court took the underlying motion under submission. Plaintiff, thus, has failed to make the showing necessary for the court to find it has jurisdiction to consider the merits of the motion. The motion is, therefore, denied.

The court declines to exercise its authority to reconsider its April 30, 2025 Order on its own motion.

Plaintiff's unopposed request for judicial notice is granted.

Order Submitted.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: M. Macadangdang; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order Re: Plaintiffs April 2025 Discovery Requests
Off Calendar Jun-24-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Walter Walker, Esq., of WALKER, HAMILTON & KEARNS LLP. Tel:415.986.3339 for plaintiff.(via Zoom Conference)
Jose Zelidon-Zepeda, Esq., of Deputy City Attorney Tel:415.355.3312 for defendant.

Hearing is held. Defendant Counsel contests certain language from the tentative ruling. The matter was discussed/ argued.The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument, the Court adopts its tentative.

DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motion For Protective Order Re: Plaintiffs April 2025 Discovery Requests.

Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for protective order is denied. The City fails to show that responding to the recent discovery propounded by the plaintiffs would cause it unwarranted annoyance, embarrassment, or oppression or undue burden and expense. That the City has already responded to a large number of discovery requests and produced a large number of documents and that this case is about an incident involving a single tree on one day is not, without more, sufficient to show that the City needs protection from responding to plaintiffs' recent discovery. Nor is the fact that in many cases the recent discovery appears to very finely parse its requests rather than posing more general requests. Nor has the City shown that plaintiffs should be limited to 35 requests for admissions and special interrogatories. If the City has already provided all responsive information to a particular request, the City can and should say so in its responses to the recent discovery. Because the City did not lack substantial justification regarding this motion, especially given its rebuffed efforts to seek a narrowing of the recent discovery, plaintiffs' request for monetary sanctions is denied.

Order is submitted.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn; Clerk: M. Macadangdang; Not Reported. (302/HEK)
